transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Ian. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess I've been feeling a bit... stuck lately. I had some plans for the weekend, you know, things I wanted to accomplish, but it just didn't go as I expected. I ended up not doing much at all, and that’s been bothering me. It's frustrating when you think you should be able to handle things, but then you can't.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you had some expectations for the weekend that didn’t quite pan out, and that’s left you feeling stuck and frustrated. It’s tough when you feel like you should be able to manage things but find yourself struggling. Can you tell me more about what you were hoping to accomplish?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I had planned to do some sewing and catch up on a few work tasks. I thought it would be a productive weekend, you know? I really enjoy sewing, and I had some ideas in mind. But when I sat down to start, it just felt overwhelming. I kept thinking, “I know how to do this; it shouldn’t be this hard.” But then I just... couldn’t focus. It’s like I was stuck in my head, and instead of getting things done, I ended up just watching TV all day. It feels disappointing, honestly.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you had some meaningful activities in mind that you enjoy, like sewing, but when it came time to start, it became overwhelming instead. That feeling of being stuck in your head and not being able to focus can be really frustrating, especially when you know you have the skills to do what you planned. It makes sense that you’d feel disappointed about not being able to accomplish what you set out to do. How did it feel for you to spend the day watching TV instead?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It felt... kind of like a letdown, honestly. I mean, I thought I’d feel better just taking a break, but instead, I just felt guilty for not doing anything productive. It’s like I was avoiding what I really wanted to do, but then I ended up feeling even worse about it. I guess I thought I could just relax and enjoy some shows, but it didn’t really help. I just kept thinking about all the things I wasn’t doing. It’s frustrating to feel that way, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you were hoping that taking a break would help you feel better, but instead, it led to feelings of guilt and frustration for not being productive. It’s tough when the very thing you think will help ends up making you feel worse. You mentioned that you felt like you were avoiding what you really wanted to do. What do you think was behind that feeling of avoidance?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think part of it is just the pressure I put on myself. I have these expectations of what I should be able to accomplish, and when I can’t meet them, it feels like I’m failing somehow. Maybe it’s a fear of not being good enough or not living up to my own standards. I don’t want to let myself down, but then when I can’t get started, it just spirals into this cycle of frustration and avoidance. It’s like I know I can do it, but when it comes down to it, I just freeze up.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure from your own expectations, and that can create a heavy weight to bear. The fear of not being good enough and the desire not to let yourself down seem to play a significant role in how you approach your tasks. It’s understandable that this pressure can lead to a cycle of frustration and avoidance, especially when you feel capable but find yourself freezing up. How do you usually cope with those feelings of pressure when they arise?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess I try to push through it sometimes, thinking that if I just start, it’ll get easier. But when that doesn’t work, I often end up distracting myself, like with TV or scrolling through my phone. It’s like a temporary escape, but it doesn’t really solve anything. I know I should probably find healthier ways to cope, but in the moment, it’s just easier to avoid the discomfort. I think I struggle with knowing how to break that cycle and actually get back on track.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you often try to push through the pressure, hoping that starting will make things feel easier, but when that doesn’t happen, you turn to distractions as a way to escape the discomfort. It’s understandable to seek out something easier in the moment, even if it doesn’t address the underlying feelings. Recognizing that you want to find healthier coping strategies is an important step. What do you think might help you begin to break that cycle and get back on track when you’re feeling overwhelmed?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve thought about that a bit. Maybe breaking things down into smaller steps could help? Like, instead of thinking about the whole sewing project or the work tasks, I could just focus on one small part at a time. I’ve heard that can make things feel less overwhelming. Also, maybe setting a timer to work for short bursts could help me stay focused without feeling like I have to commit to a long stretch. But I guess it’s just a matter of actually trying those things when I’m in that moment of feeling stuck. It’s easier said than done, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Breaking things down into smaller steps and using a timer for short bursts of focus sounds like a thoughtful approach. It seems like you’re recognizing that taking smaller, manageable actions might help reduce the feeling of overwhelm. I can see how it would be challenging to implement those strategies when you’re in the moment of feeling stuck. What do you think might support you in actually trying those ideas when you’re feeling that way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: That’s a good question. I think maybe having a plan or a reminder could help. Like, if I write down those smaller steps and keep them visible, it might prompt me to actually follow through. Also, maybe setting a specific time to start could create a little structure. I guess having some accountability, whether it’s just checking in with myself or even talking to someone about it, could motivate me to try those strategies instead of just defaulting to distractions. It’s just figuring out how to make that happen when I’m feeling overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having a visible plan and some structure could really help you when you’re feeling overwhelmed. Writing down those smaller steps and setting specific times to start might provide that extra nudge you need. The idea of accountability, whether through self-check-ins or sharing with someone else, seems like it could be a motivating factor as well. It’s understandable that figuring out how to implement these strategies in the moment can be tricky. What do you think would be the first step you could take to create that plan or structure for yourself?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the first step could be to take a little time to sit down and write out a list of the smaller tasks I want to accomplish. Just breaking everything down into bite-sized pieces. I could also maybe set aside a specific time this week to do that, so it feels more intentional. Once I have that list, I could try to pick one task to focus on each day, maybe even using a timer to keep it manageable. It feels like a good starting point, but I guess I’ll have to see if I can actually follow through with it when the time comes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid first step, Ian. Taking the time to write out a list of smaller tasks and setting a specific time to do that can help create a sense of intention and clarity. It’s great that you’re thinking about picking one task to focus on each day and using a timer to keep it manageable. It’s completely normal to feel uncertain about following through, especially when you’re feeling overwhelmed. How do you think you might remind yourself of this plan when you’re in that moment of feeling stuck?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could set a reminder on my phone or maybe even write it down on a sticky note and put it somewhere I’ll see it often, like on my desk or by my sewing machine. Just something to jog my memory about the plan and the smaller steps I laid out. I could also try to remind myself that it’s okay to start small and that I don’t have to tackle everything at once. Maybe even having a friend check in with me could help keep me accountable. It’s just about finding those little nudges to keep me on track when I start to feel overwhelmed again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are great ideas, Ian. Setting reminders on your phone or using sticky notes can serve as helpful nudges to keep your plan front of mind. It’s also really important to remind yourself that starting small is perfectly okay, and it sounds like you’re already recognizing that. Having a friend check in with you for accountability could provide that extra support you might need when things get tough. It seems like you’re developing a thoughtful approach to navigating those overwhelming moments. How are you feeling about this plan as you think it through?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. Just talking through it and thinking about those small steps makes it seem more manageable. I guess there’s a part of me that still feels a little anxious about whether I’ll actually follow through, but having a plan in place feels better than just feeling stuck. It’s nice to think that I might have some tools to help me when I start to feel overwhelmed again. I just hope I can remember to use them when the time comes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that discussing your plan has made it feel more manageable. It’s completely natural to feel some anxiety about following through, but having a plan and tools in place can provide a sense of empowerment. Remembering to use those tools when you’re feeling overwhelmed can be a challenge, but it sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success with reminders and support. What do you think might help you reinforce that hope and remind yourself of the progress you’re making as you move forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think keeping a little journal could help. Just jotting down my thoughts, feelings, and any progress I make, even if it’s small, might reinforce that hope. It could be a way to reflect on what’s working and what isn’t, and it might remind me that I’m taking steps, even if they feel small. I could also celebrate those little wins, like completing a task or sticking to my plan for a day. I guess recognizing progress, no matter how minor, could help me stay motivated and remind me that I’m moving forward, even when it feels slow.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Keeping a journal sounds like a fantastic idea! It could provide you with a space to reflect on your thoughts and feelings, track your progress, and celebrate those small wins. Acknowledging even the minor achievements can be a powerful motivator and help reinforce that sense of hope you’re cultivating. It’s great that you’re thinking about how to recognize your progress, as that can really shift your perspective over time. As you consider starting this journaling practice, what do you think would be the best way to incorporate it into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might be helpful to set aside a specific time each day to write in my journal, maybe in the evening when I can reflect on my day. I could make it part of my wind-down routine, like after dinner or before bed. That way, it becomes a regular habit. I could start with just a few minutes, writing down what I accomplished, how I felt, and anything I want to remember. It feels like a good way to process my thoughts and keep track of my progress. I just hope I can stick to it and make it a consistent part of my routine.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Setting aside a specific time each evening to write in your journal sounds like a wonderful way to create a regular habit. Incorporating it into your wind-down routine could help make it feel like a natural part of your day. Starting with just a few minutes is a great approach, as it keeps it manageable and allows you to process your thoughts and reflect on your progress. It’s understandable to have some concerns about sticking to it, but having that intention and structure in place can really support you. What do you think might help you stay committed to this journaling practice as you start?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe setting a reminder on my phone could help, just to nudge me to take that time for myself. I could also try to create a cozy space for journaling, like having a nice spot with good lighting and maybe a cup of tea or something, to make it feel special. I guess having that little ritual could make it more inviting. And if I miss a day, I’ll try to remind myself that it’s okay and just get back to it the next day. It’s about progress, not perfection, right?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are excellent strategies, Ian! Setting a reminder on your phone can provide that gentle nudge to prioritize your journaling time. Creating a cozy space with good lighting and a comforting drink sounds like a lovely way to make the experience inviting and special. It’s also very wise to remind yourself that if you miss a day, it’s okay to just get back to it the next day. Focusing on progress rather than perfection can help ease some of that pressure. It seems like you’re building a thoughtful and supportive routine for yourself. How are you feeling about all these ideas as you think about putting them into practice?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a lot more optimistic about it all. Just having a plan and thinking through these ideas makes it seem less daunting. I like that I’m creating a routine that feels supportive rather than adding more pressure. I know it won’t be perfect, but I’m starting to feel like I have some tools to help me navigate those tough moments. It’s nice to think that I can take small steps and still make progress. I guess I’m just hoping I can remember to be kind to myself along the way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling more optimistic and that you’re creating a supportive routine for yourself. Acknowledging that it won’t be perfect and focusing on small steps is such a healthy perspective. It’s great that you’re also emphasizing the importance of being kind to yourself throughout this process. That self-compassion can really make a difference when navigating challenges. As you move forward, what do you think might help you maintain that kindness toward yourself, especially during tougher moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think reminding myself that it’s okay to struggle sometimes would be important. I could try to practice some self-compassion, maybe by talking to myself the way I would to a friend who’s going through a tough time. It might help to have some affirmations or positive statements I can refer back to when I’m feeling down. Also, taking breaks and allowing myself to rest without feeling guilty could be key. Just recognizing that it’s part of the process and that I’m doing my best, even when things don’t go as planned, might help me maintain that kindness.
